title: The Grade 4 Leap: Fractions and Large Numbers
excerpt: Grade 4 is called the filtering grade, and not for nothing. The real jump from grade 3, which topics arrive, and how to prepare so your child does not break down mid-year.
author: Smart Worksheets Team
content:
Why grade 4 trips up so many kids

Teachers call grade 4 the filtering grade, and there is a reason. Up to grade 3, most math was concrete: counting, adding, multiplying things you can picture. In grade 4 everything jumps a level into the abstract. Fractions, decimals, long division, large numbers. A child whose foundation is shaky suddenly feels the ground giving way, not because they are not smart, but because the leap is real.

What arrives this year

  • Fractions. What a fraction is, how to compare them, and how to add fractions with a common denominator. This is the most demanding topic of the year.
  • Long division. The orderly method for dividing large numbers, which leans entirely on a solid grip of the times tables.
  • Decimals. The start of understanding that 0.5 and one half are really the same thing.
  • Large numbers. Adding, subtracting, and multiplying three- and four-digit numbers, with carrying and borrowing.

How to prepare for it

The secret is to strengthen the foundation before reaching the new topic. Two things are worth gold. First, automatic times tables. Without them, both long division and fractions become torture, because every middle step demands a pause. Second, confident addition and subtraction with carrying and borrowing, because they come back in every new topic.

If your child enters grade 4 with these two solid, most of the leap is no longer frightening. If not, it is worth investing in them in the summer beforehand, rather than waiting for the teacher to race ahead.

The mistake parents make

A parent sees the child struggling with fractions and drills more and more fractions. But sometimes the root is actually a missing times table. Find out exactly where your child breaks down, and reinforce the underlying operation, not just the topic on the page.

A small example from the year: one half plus one quarter

To feel the leap, take a typical grade 4 fractions problem. How much is one half plus one quarter? A child who tries to add across the top and the bottom gets 2 over 6, which is wrong. The right way is to find a common denominator. One half is two quarters, so two quarters plus one quarter equals three quarters. That is exactly the new abstract thinking of grade 4, which is why it is worth practicing slowly and with a drawing before moving on to dry problems.

Questions parents ask

My child was excellent until now and suddenly struggles, what happened? Most likely the jump to the abstract. It is very common and does not mean something went wrong. It takes a little patience and some shoring up of the foundation.

How much should we practice? Fifteen focused minutes a day on the weak spot beats an hour of general work. Quality before quantity.

Where do we start? It is best to first lock in the times tables in our multiplication generator, and in parallel begin fractions with our fractions generator and the grade 4 practice page.
